data.description:

  • A key differentiator is that 18F-FET imaging like TLX101-Px is already referenced in international glioma guidelines, yet no FDA-approved targeted amino acid PET agent for brain cancer imaging currently exists in the U.S., highlighting a potential first-mover position that could also support Telix’s LAT1-targeted therapy program.

The TLX101-Px resubmission speaks directly to that story, but the key near term catalyst still sits in execution across the wider pipeline and U.S. roll out, while the biggest risk remains ongoing heavy investment in R&D and manufacturing that already weighs on earnings.

The recent cyclotron order with IBA for the RLS radiopharmacy network ties directly into TLX101-Px and the broader imaging portfolio, since reliable in house isotope supply is essential to scaling any approved 18F-FET agent. data.title: Has York Space Systems (YSS) Fallen Too Far After Recent Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether York Space Systems at around US$17.68 offers value or just extra risk, the key is to separate short term noise from what the current price actually implies.
  • The share price has recently been under pressure, with a 7 day return of 11.5% decline, a 30 day move of 36.9% decline, and a year to date return of 47.4% decline. This naturally raises questions about whether sentiment has swung too far.
  • Recent news coverage has focused on York Space Systems as part of the broader interest in space and defense related businesses, including commentary on funding conditions, contract pipelines, and the competitive environment for satellite platforms. This backdrop helps explain why the market has been quick to reassess risk when headlines shift, even without company specific earnings statements driving the latest moves.
  • Right now, York Space Systems has a valuation score of 2 out of 6. The next sections will walk through what different valuation methods say about the stock and then finish with a broader framework that can help you judge value more clearly than any single metric.

York Space Systems scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: York Space Systems Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today, aiming to estimate what the whole business could be worth right now based on those cash flows.

For York Space Systems, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $20.7 million. Analyst and model projections then step this out over the coming years, with ten year estimates reaching $255.7 million of free cash flow in 2035. Figures for 2026 to 2030 come from analyst inputs. The later years are extrapolated by Simply Wall St using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity framework.

When all of those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model suggests an estimated intrinsic value of about $33.45 per share. Compared with a current share price around $17.68, this output implies the shares are trading at about a 47.2% discount to that cash flow based estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: York Space Systems Price vs Sales

For companies where profits are limited or volatile, the P/S ratio is often more useful than P/E, because it compares the share price with revenue rather than earnings. Investors usually accept a higher P/S ratio when they expect stronger growth and lower risk, and look for a lower P/S when growth is uncertain or risks feel higher.

York Space Systems currently trades on a P/S of 6.28x. This sits above both its Aerospace & Defense industry average P/S of 4.60x and the peer group average of 4.08x, which suggests the market is putting a higher value on each dollar of York’s sales than it does for many rivals.

data.title: Intuitive Machines (LUNR) Q4 Loss Of US$19.9 Million Tests Profitability Narrative
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (LUNR) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$44.8 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.17, alongside a net loss of US$19.9 million, while on a trailing 12 month basis revenue was US$210.1 million and EPS stood at a loss of US$0.71 with a net loss of US$83.9 million. Over recent quarters, the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$62.5 million in Q1 2025 to US$50.3 million in Q2, US$52.4 million in Q3 and US$44.8 million in Q4, with EPS losses ranging between US$0.06 and US$0.22 across the period. Taken together, the latest print keeps the story centered on scaling revenue while working to tighten loss-making margins.

See our full analysis for Intuitive Machines.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the dominant bull and bear narratives around Intuitive Machines and where those narratives might need a reset.

See what the community is saying about Intuitive Machines

Advertisement

TTM losses of US$83.9 million keep profitability in focus

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Intuitive Machines booked US$210.1 million in revenue against a net loss of US$83.9 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.71, which sits alongside five year losses that have grown at about 53.5% per year.
  • Critics highlight in the bearish narrative that persistent negative margins and heavy dependence on large government contracts could keep earnings under pressure, and the current loss profile lines up with that concern as quarterly net loss moved between US$7 million and US$25.3 million through FY 2025 while still relying heavily on mission and program awards to convert backlog into revenue.
    • The move from a Q4 2024 net loss of US$149.3 million to a Q4 2025 net loss of US$19.9 million shows that recent period results differ markedly from earlier TTM loss levels, which both bulls and bears will likely factor into their views on how quickly margins might improve.
    • Bears also point to the capital intensive model and customer concentration risk, and the trailing 12 month loss of US$83.9 million alongside ongoing investments highlighted in the narratives gives that risk a clear numerical anchor for anyone watching the path to profitability.
On this view, skeptics may feel recent earnings still leave little room for error before contract delays or cost overruns start to bite into already negative margins, and they may look to how management talks about funding and contract diversification in future updates before getting more comfortable with the long term earnings path.🐻 Intuitive Machines Bear Case

High growth forecasts vs current P/S of 10.7x

  • Forecasts in the data call for revenue growth of about 29.2% per year and earnings growth of about 55.77% per year with profitability expected within three years, yet the stock is currently trading on a P/S of 10.7x compared with peer and industry averages of 5.2x and 4.6x.
  • Supporters in the bullish narrative argue that leadership in lunar missions and deep space infrastructure plus vertical integration could justify paying this higher multiple, but the current P/S premium means those growth expectations already carry a price tag that investors need to be comfortable underwriting.
    • The same narrative talks about revenue potentially reaching around US$519.5 million and earnings of US$39.4 million by 2028, which is a large step up from the trailing 12 month revenue base of US$210.1 million and loss of US$83.9 million, so any slowdown versus the projected 30% plus growth path could leave the P/S ratio exposed.
    • Analysts in the bullish cohort also assume margins move from roughly negative triple digit levels today to positive mid single digit or better in a few years, so the current valuation effectively leans on a shift from loss making to profitable that has not yet appeared in the historical EPS trend of quarterly losses between US$0.06 and US$0.22 per share in FY 2025.
If you are weighing the bullish case, it really comes down to whether those growth and margin targets justify paying roughly double the peer and industry P/S while the company is still in loss making territory today.🐂 Intuitive Machines Bull Case

Volatile share price alongside a US$49.46 DCF fair value

  • The stock trades at US$18.91 against a DCF fair value in the data of US$49.46, implying a large modeled gap, at the same time as the risk summary flags high share price volatility in the past three months plus dilution and insider selling over the past year.
  • Consensus narrative views this kind of set up as a trade off between a potential valuation gap and execution plus sentiment risks, and the combination of a modeled fair value well above the current price with a P/S multiple already ahead of peers shows how sharply opinions can split.
    • On one side, the roughly 61.8% discount to the DCF fair value and forecast revenue growth of about 29.2% per year appeal to investors who put more weight on long term contract potential and expected profitability within three years than on recent volatility.
    • On the other side, the same data highlights shareholder dilution and recent insider selling, which some investors treat as warning signs that can add pressure in a stock that is already described as highly volatile and still posting an annual loss of US$83.9 million on US$210.1 million of revenue.

Next Steps

  • Bears who focus on that five year decline get some backing from the numbers, because
    • quarterly EPS has swung from a loss of C¥0.01399 in Q4 2024 to C¥0.021694 in Q4 2025, and between C¥0.003 and C¥0.028598 within FY 2025, highlighting that profitability has not been smooth over time,
